steps.title: Spinal Cord Compression
steps.text: What is the most appropriate first step in managing a child with suspected spinal cord compression at the primary level?
steps.rationale: At the primary level, stabilizing vital functions (ABCs) is the immediate priority before further interventions or referral.

steps.title: Spinal Cord Compression
steps.text: Which intravenous fluid is appropriate to initiate in a child with suspected spinal cord compression at the primary care level?
steps.rationale: This combination helps support perfusion and glucose needs while awaiting definitive care at a higher-level facility.

steps.title: Superior Vena Cava Compressions Syndrome
steps.text: What is the most appropriate patient position for a child suspected of having Superior Vena Cava (SVC) Compression Syndrome?
steps.rationale: Lying flat can worsen respiratory distress in SVC syndrome. Elevating the upper body improves venous drainage and reduces upper body congestion.

steps.title: Superior Vena Cava Compressions Syndrome
steps.text: A child presents with facial swelling, distended neck veins, and difficulty breathing. What is the most appropriate next step after securing the airway, breathing, and circulation?
steps.rationale: Tertiary care is needed for imaging, diagnosis, and definitive treatment (e.g., chemotherapy, surgery, or radiotherapy). Early referral prevents delays in life-saving interventions.

textChoices.text: Refer urgently to a tertiary centre
Place the child in a supine position

steps.title: Hyperleukocytosis
steps.text: What is the most appropriate initial fluid management for a child with suspected hyperleukocytosis at the primary care level?
steps.rationale: In the primary care setting, it is critical to initiate supportive care promptly. Starting IV Normal Saline ensures hydration while avoiding potassium-containing fluids, which could worsen metabolic complications in hyperleukocytosis.

textChoices.text: Start IV Normal Saline and add potassium chloride
Start IV Normal Saline and avoid potassium-containing fluids

steps.title: Wilms Tumour / Nephroblastoma
steps.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate primary care approach for a child with suspected Wilms tumour (nephroblastoma)?
steps.rationale: In primary care, the role is limited to suspecting the diagnosis, providing initial counselling to the family, and ensuring prompt referral to higher-level facilities for confirmation and further management. Delay in referral may worsen outcomes.

textChoices.text: Start chemotherapy immediately at the primary care level
Perform abdominal ultrasound to confirm the diagnosis
Provide appropriate counselling and refer the patient early
Observe the patient for 2 weeks before making a referral

steps.title: Retinoblastoma
steps.text: What is the most appropriate next step at the primary level when a child presents with leukocoria (white pupillary reflex)?
steps.rationale: At the primary level, a child with suspected retinoblastoma (e.g., leukocoria) must be urgently referred after counselling and discussion with an ophthalmologist at a secondary facility.

textChoices.text: Start topical antibiotics and monitor for improvement
Counsel the caregiver, discuss with ophthalmology
Prescribe vitamin A and review in two weeks
Reassure the family and advise regular eye checks

steps.title: Sickle cell disease
steps.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line action in a child presenting with a sickle cell pain crisis at the primary care level?
steps.rationale: At the primary level, the immediate goal in managing a sickle cell crisis is to relieve pain and address any infections, which are common triggers. More advanced interventions are managed at higher levels of care.

textChoices.text: Refer directly to tertiary care without treatment
Manage pain and treat underlying infections

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: Which of the following is an appropriate step in managing anaemia at the primary care level?
steps.rationale: At the primary level, management is supportive and focuses on treating common reversible causes like infections and parasitic infestations. Blood transfusion and advanced diagnostics require referral to a higher level.

steps.title: Anaemia
steps.text: Which haemoglobin (Hb) level defines anaemia in children under 6 years old according to the guideline?
steps.rationale: The guideline defines anaemia in young children as Hb <9.3 g/dL (roughly Hct <27%).
